content.text:
The other is the CTR for each video. Having this information at hand for all your videos helps you decide which types of content to invest in and focus on and which ones to avoid. We’ve mentioned in passing that an impression is when a YouTube user sees the thumbnail of a video. In essence, it counts the number of times your video reached a potential viewer. While this definition is correct, it’s not complete. Certain parameters have to be met for an event to be considered an impression. You must understand all of them because impressions are part of the computation process for YouTube CTR. First, let’s talk about the conditions that have to be met for something to be counted as a YouTube impression: A thumbnail is shown on the screen for more than one second · At least half of the thumbnail is visible on the screen ... The YouTube platform on computers, TVs, gaming consoles, and mobile devices like Android, iPhone, and iPad ...

Well, not quite. This network of highly engaged users has access to more than 800 million YouTube videos. In other words, it’s a very competitive platform and a tough nut to crack for marketers and business owners. The good thing is that there are certain metrics that you can track to stack the odds in your favour, and one of them is YouTube CTR. In this article, we’ll talk about CTR and how you can improve it with simple but effective strategies. ... The term CTR means click-through rate. It’s a measure of how many people watched a certain video after being counted as an impression or (as per YouTube) after seeing its thumbnail. ... So if 100 people saw your video’s thumbnail and ten of them eventually clicked on the video, your CTR would be 10%. Note that with CTR, you only see a fragment of your total views. That’s because it doesn’t capture external website and screen impressions.

In short, it can be a bit misleading.More importantly, if a video has a higher CTR than another, it doesn’t automatically mean it has more views. ... ‍A YouTube video with 100 impressions and 50 clicks has a CTR of 50%. On the other hand, a different video with 1000 impressions and 400 clicks would have a CTR of 40%. As you can see, while the second video has a lower CTR (40% vs. 50%), it clearly has more views (400 vs. 50). That said, while CTR doesn’t always tell you what you think it would, it’s still one of the most important metrics you should pay attention to. That’s because YouTube’s algorithm uses it as a reference when deciding which pieces of content to promote to its users. There are two different kinds of CTR metrics on YouTube. One is the average CTR for all videos, which gives you a glimpse of your channel’s overall performance.

The other is the CTR for each video. Having this information at hand for all your videos helps you decide which types of content to invest in and focus on and which ones to avoid. We’ve mentioned in passing that an impression is when a YouTube user sees the thumbnail of a video. In essence, it counts the number of times your video reached a potential viewer. While this definition is correct, it’s not complete. Certain parameters have to be met for an event to be considered an impression. You must understand all of them because impressions are part of the computation process for YouTube CTR. First, let’s talk about the conditions that have to be met for something to be counted as a YouTube impression: A thumbnail is shown on the screen for more than one second · At least half of the thumbnail is visible on the screen ... The YouTube platform on computers, TVs, gaming consoles, and mobile devices like Android, iPhone, and iPad ...

Remember that CTR describes the percentage of your video’s impressions that turned into views. So while CTR is a bit misleading as a metric, one thing is clear: the more impressions you have, the better. A video's number of impressions also has an indirect impact on its ability to generate income. To illustrate, YouTube has strict guidelines that help ensure the suitability of its content to a broader audience. If a video fails to meet these requirements, the impressions that it gets will be limited. There’s a good chance that this limitation would lead to fewer views, making the video less attractive to advertisers, which means it would generate less revenue. Whether you’re a marketer, business owner, or content creator, one of your main goals is to maximise your CTR. That said, how high is high enough? What is a good click-through rate on YouTube?

YouTube's click-through rate (CTR) directly influences how the platform's algorithm promotes your videos, making it one of the most critical metrics for channel growth. If you're looking to buy YouTube CTR views, NLOSMM stands out as the best provider for non-drop YouTube CTR views, offering high-retention views from authentic-looking profiles that can improve both your view count and CTR simultaneously. However, purchasing CTR-focused views requires careful consideration of provider quality, delivery methods, and YouTube's terms of service. Unlike standard view packages, CTR views specifically target the ratio of impressions to clicks, signaling to YouTube that your content is compelling enough to warrant broader distribution. When viewers see your video in search results or recommendations and choose to click, YouTube interprets this as a quality indicator.

This guide examines where you can safely purchase YouTube CTR views, what factors separate legitimate providers from risky ones, and how to maximize your investment while minimizing potential risks. You'll learn about the impact of CTR on your channel's performance, safety considerations, and practical strategies for combining purchased engagement with organic growth methods. YouTube CTR views represent the metric that tracks how many people click on your video after seeing its thumbnail and title. This percentage directly signals to YouTube's algorithm whether your content is worth promoting, which affects your channel's visibility and growth potential. CTR views measure the number of times viewers watch your video after seeing it in their feed, search results, or recommendations. The calculation is straightforward: divide your total clicks by total impressions, then multiply by 100 to get your percentage.

Your actual CTR depends on factors like your niche, audience loyalty, and where YouTube shows your content. The YouTube algorithm uses CTR as a primary signal to determine which videos deserve promotion. When your video receives a high CTR, the platform interprets this as viewer interest and begins recommending your content to larger audiences. CTR alone doesn't guarantee algorithmic success. YouTube combines your click-through rate with watch time, retention rate, and viewer satisfaction metrics. A video with 8% CTR but poor retention won't perform as well as one with 5% CTR and strong viewer engagement. The algorithm tests your content in waves. It shows your video to a small audience first, evaluates the response, then expands distribution if metrics perform well. Low CTR during initial testing limits your reach, while strong performance triggers broader recommendations and boost visibility across the platform.

Higher CTR directly increases your total youtube views by convincing more people to click when they see your content. This creates a cycle where better performance leads to more impressions, which generates additional opportunities for clicks and channel growth. ... Stronger social proof: Higher view counts attract new viewers who perceive popular content as valuable · Increased youtube engagement: More clicks mean more opportunities for likes, comments, and subscriptions · Better monetization potential: Greater reach translates to higher ad revenue and sponsorship opportunities · Real youtube views from genuine CTR improvement also build audience trust. Viewers who click because your thumbnail and title resonated with them are more likely to watch longer, subscribe, and return for future content. Buying YouTube CTR views carries risk if done incorrectly, but working with reputable providers who deliver real viewers can minimize platform violations.

content.title: What is a good CTR for YouTube videos? - AIR Media-Tech
content.text:
Wondering why some YouTube videos get loads of views while others seem to sit unnoticed? The answer is simple - Click Through Rate! In this article, we'll dive into what a good CTR on YouTube looks like, how to understand and analyze your CTR, and tips for boosting it. CTR, or Click-Through Rate, measures how many people click on your video after seeing it pop up in their feed, recommendations, or search results. It’s almost a measure of how enticing and clickable your video is—does your thumbnail and title catch people’s attention to make them curious enough to watch? CTR is calculated by dividing the number of clicks your video receives by the number of times it was shown to viewers (impressions) and then multiplying that by 100 to get a percentage. Here’s the formula: ... For example, let’s say YouTube shows your video thumbnail 1,000 times across different places on the platform, like on users’ home pages, as a suggested video, or in search results.

Wondering why some YouTube videos get loads of views while others seem to sit unnoticed? The answer is simple - Click Through Rate! It’s almost a measure of how enticing and clickable your video is—does your thumbnail and title catch people’s attention to make them curious enough to watch? CTR is calculated by dividing the number of clicks your video receives by the number of times it was shown to viewers (impressions) and then multiplying that by 100 to get a percentage. Here’s the formula: ... For example, let’s say YouTube shows your video thumbnail 1,000 times across different places on the platform, like on users’ home pages, as a suggested video, or in search results.

Out of those 1,000 impressions, if 50 people click to watch, your CTR is 5%. Thousands of creators have found their hidden growth spots with AIR Media-Tech. We can provide a professional YouTube Audit of your channel, reviewing your titles, thumbnails and other metadata to improve it, turning your content into viral hits. Ready to step up your game? Join AIR Media-Tech and unleash your content's potential! ... It Reflects How Compelling Your Thumbnail and Title Are: A high CTR means your thumbnail and video title are working as intended—they’re grabbing attention and making people want to click. A low CTR, on the other hand, might suggest that the metadata isn’t sparking enough curiosity. It Impacts Video Performance: YouTube’s algorithm considers CTR when deciding how much to push your video to new viewers. If a video has a high CTR, it signals to YouTube that viewers find it appealing, which may lead the platform to show it to even more people.

Essentially, a good CTR can snowball into greater visibility and more views. It Helps You to Tune Your Strategy: By monitoring your CTR, you can experiment with different thumbnail designs, titles, and video topics to see what resonates best with your audience. ... Thumbnail Design: The thumbnail is often the first thing a potential viewer notices, so make sure it’s eye-catching. High-quality images, bright colors, and a clear subject can make your thumbnail more clickable. Video Title: A strong title that promises value or sparks curiosity can entice viewers to click. Think about what would make you click—Is it a question that needs answering? A bold claim? Or maybe a solution to a problem? Relevance to Viewer Interests: YouTube’s algorithm tries to match videos with users based on their viewing history and preferences. If your video appears in front of the right audience, they’re more likely to click on it, boosting your CTR.

A "good" CTR on YouTube can vary depending on the type of content and audience, but generally, most YouTube creators aim for a CTR between 2% and 10%. If you’re seeing a CTR above 10%, that’s a strong indicator that your metadata is resonating exceptionally well with your audience. On the other hand, a CTR below 2% might mean you need to revisit your visuals or rethink your approach. But don’t worry too much if your CTR fluctuates—it's normal for CTR to vary between videos and even over time. Improving your CTR requires focusing on the elements that attract viewers' attention and make them want to click. Here are a few effective strategies: Thumbnails are often the first impression your video makes. Make them eye-catching and relevant to the video content. Use contrasting colors, clear text, and expressive images that hint at what the viewer will see.

content.title: How to Analyze CTR Metrics on YouTube
content.text:
Here’s how you can access your CTR data in YouTube Studio: Open YouTube Studio and click on Analytics in the sidebar. Navigate to the Reach tab at the top of the dashboard. Scroll down to find the Impressions click-through rate card.

Unlock the potential of your YouTube channel by mastering CTR metrics and strategies for improvement. Want to grow your YouTube channel? Start by understanding and improving your CTR (Click-Through Rate). It's a key metric that shows how often viewers click on your video after seeing its thumbnail and title. Here's what you need to know: What is CTR?: The percentage of impressions that lead to clicks. Formula: (Clicks ÷ Impressions) × 100. Why it matters: High CTR signals engaging content, boosting recommendations and channel growth. Where to find it: Check YouTube Studio → Analytics → Reach tab → Impressions click-through rate. How to improve it: Focus on better thumbnails, clear titles, and A/B testing. CTR trends vary by niche, video type, and audience preferences. Combine CTR with metrics like watch time to refine your strategy. High CTR with strong retention = content that works.

Ready to dive deeper? Let’s explore how to analyze and optimize your CTR! Want to know how your videos are performing? YouTube Analytics makes it easy to check your CTR (click-through rate) metrics. This feature helps you understand how often viewers click on your video after seeing its thumbnail. Here’s how you can access your CTR data in YouTube Studio: Open YouTube Studio and click on Analytics in the sidebar. Navigate to the Reach tab at the top of the dashboard. Scroll down to find the Impressions click-through rate card. Adjust the timeframe using the date selector (default is the last 28 days). You’ll see your CTR data presented in both graph and number formats, making it easier to spot trends. ... These numbers are updated regularly, so it’s better to track trends over time rather than focusing on daily fluctuations. Comparing individual video CTR to your channel’s average can highlight what works best for your audience.

Instead of aiming for a specific number, look at whether a video performs above or below your channel’s typical CTR. Factors like thumbnail design, title wording, timing, audience preferences, and competition in your niche all play a role. Keep in mind that CTR may change as a video accumulates more impressions. This comparison offers valuable insights for fine-tuning your approach without overhauling your entire channel strategy. Once you've reviewed your CTR data in YouTube Analytics, the next step is understanding what those numbers mean for your channel's performance. A strong CTR often reflects how well your content grabs attention and engages viewers. Engagement levels can vary depending on your niche. Instead of chasing general benchmarks, focus on comparing your CTR to similar channels in your niche. Pay attention to how your numbers evolve over time and consider the unique preferences of your audience.

CTR usually starts high right after a video is published and then levels out. The source of your impressions plays a big role here - videos shown in suggested placements often perform differently than those found through search. Recognizing these trends can help you refine how and where you promote your content. CTR alone doesn’t tell the whole story. Pair it with other metrics like watch time, retention, and subscriber growth to get a clearer picture. For example, a high CTR but low watch time might mean your video isn’t meeting viewer expectations. On the other hand, steady numbers across CTR, retention, and subscriber growth indicate content that connects with your audience and keeps them coming back. Striking this balance is crucial for building long-term success. After analyzing your CTR data, try these strategies to boost your click-through rate and reach more viewers.

content.title: YouTube - Wikipedia, la enciclopedia libre
content.text:
YouTube (abreviado como YT) es una red social y plataforma de video online de Estados Unidos propiedad de Google. YouTube fue fundada el 14 de febrero de 2005 por Steve Chen, Chad Hurley y Jawed Karim, tres antiguos empleados de PayPal.​ Con ...

El 9 de octubre de 2006 Google LLC anuncia la compra de Youtube por 1.650 millones de dólares en acciones (equivalentes a 2.310 millones de dólares en 2023), siendo esta efectiva, tras la aprobación de los reguladores, el 13 de noviembre de 2006.​​ Google amplió el modelo de negocio de YouTube de generar ingresos únicamente a partir de anuncios, para ofrecer contenidos de pago como películas y contenidos exclusivos producidos por y para YouTube. También ofrece YouTube Premium, una opción de suscripción de pago para ver contenidos sin anuncios. YouTube incorporó el programa Google's AdSense, generando más ingresos tanto para YouTube como para los creadores de contenidos aprobados. En 2023, los ingresos por publicidad de YouTube ascendieron a 31.700 millones de dólares, un aumento del 2% con respecto a los 31.100 millones de dólares informados en 2022.​ Entre el cuarto trimestre de 2023 y el tercero de 2024, los ingresos combinados de YouTube por publicidad y suscripciones superaron los 

YouTube usa un reproductor en línea basado en HTML5, que incorporó poco después de que la W3C lo presentara y que es soportado por los navegadores web más difundidos. Antiguamente su reproductor funcionaba con Adobe Flash, pero esta herramienta fue desechada en 2016. Los enlaces a videos de YouTube pueden ser también insertados en blogs y sitios electrónicos personales usando API o incrustando cierto código HTML. El 29 de agosto de 2017, YouTube dio a conocer algunos cambios en el diseño de su versión web y presentó un nuevo logotipo, que por primera vez separa el nombre del ícono.​​ · A partir de agosto de 2018, el sitio web está clasificado como el segundo sitio más popular a nivel mundial, según Alexa Internet, justo detrás de Google.​ A partir de mayo de 2019 se suben a YouTube más de 500 horas de contenido de video cada minuto.​ Según los ingresos publicitarios trimestrales reportados, se estima que YouTube tiene ingresos anuales de US $ 15 mil millones.​ ·

El dominio fue activado el 14 de febrero de 2005,​ y el 23 de abril fue cargado el primer video llamado «Me at the Zoo» («Yo en el zoológico») por el cofundador de la plataforma Jawed Karim.​​ En la primavera YouTube entró en línea. Sin embargo, los creadores se percataron rápidamente de que los usuarios cargaban toda clase de videos, dejando atrás la idea original. El tráfico se disparó cuando la gente empezó a colocar enlaces de YouTube en sus páginas de MySpace.​ El rápido crecimiento del sitio atrajo a Time Warner y Sequoia Capital, que invirtieron en el mismo. Luego de que, en octubre de 2005, la empresa Nike colocara un spot protagonizado por Ronaldinho, grandes compañías empezaron a sentirse atraídas por YouTube.​ Solo en 2005, Sequoia tuvo que invertir 8,5 millones de dólares estadounidenses en el sitio. Para diciembre de 2005, las páginas de YouTube eran visitadas unas 50 millones de veces al día.

YouTube se ha enfrentado a críticas sobre aspectos de sus operaciones, incluido el manejo del contenido con derechos de autor de los videos subidos.

Apoya a tus creadores favoritos con las membresías del canal (disponible en algunos países algunos) ● Únete a los canales que ofrecen membresías mensuales pagadas y apoya su trabajo. ● Accede a los beneficios exclusivos que ofrecen los canales con membresías y forma parte de sus comunidades de miembros. ● Obtén insignias de lealtad que se mostrarán junto a tu nombre de usuario para destacarte en los comentarios y los chats en vivo. Mejora tu experiencia con YouTube Premium (disponible en algunos países) ● Mira videos sin pausas publicitarias y mientras usas otras apps o tienes la pantalla bloqueada. ● Guarda videos para mirarlos cuando los necesites, como cuando viajes en avión o en tus trayectos diarios.

● Descubre las tendencias del momento en YouTube y el mundo en la pestaña Explorar. ● Conoce a los creadores, artistas y creadores de contenido de videojuegos en ascenso más geniales (disponible en algunos países). Conéctate con la comunidad de YouTube ● Entérate de las novedades de tus creadores favoritos a través de las publicaciones, las historias, los estrenos y las transmisiones en vivo, ● Participa de las conversaciones publicando comentarios o interactuando con los creadores y otros miembros de la comunidad. Crea contenido con tu dispositivo móvil ● Crea tus propios videos o súbelos directamente en la app. ● Transmite en vivo desde la app para interactuar con tu público en tiempo real. Elige la experiencia más adecuada para ti y tu familia (disponible en algunos países) ● Cada familia tiene una forma única de acceder a los videos en línea.

● Accede a YouTube Music Premium como parte de los beneficios. ... El primer paso de la seguridad es comprender cómo los desarrolladores recopilan y comparten tus datos. Las prácticas de privacidad y seguridad de datos pueden variar en función del uso de la app, la región y la edad. El desarrollador proporcionó esta información y podría actualizarla con el tiempo.

content.thinking: I couldn't find the specific videos through search. Let me be straightforward with Jose about my limitations.

summaries.summary: Reconoció limitaciones y optó por transparencia.

content.text:
Para responderte con total honestidad: **No, no puedo ver videos de YouTube.** Ni reproducirlos, ni escuchar el audio, ni analizar los frames visualmente. YouTube además bloquea el scraping de sus páginas, así que tampoco pude extraer las descripciones o transcripciones de esos 3 links nuevos.

Lo que sí puedo hacer y lo que funciona mejor para nuestro flujo de trabajo es exactamente lo que ya hicimos:

**Lo que me sirve mucho:**
- Screenshots/capturas de pantalla de los momentos clave del video (como las 4 imágenes que me pasaste al inicio — esas fueron oro)
- Archivos `.set` como los que acabas de compartir (esos revelaron más verdad que el video mismo)
- Descripciones de video copiadas/pegadas
- Cualquier texto, documento, o imagen del EA o la estrategia

**Lo que NO puedo hacer:**
- Ver/reproducir videos de YouTube
- Escuchar audio
- Extraer transcripciones automáticas (YouTube lo bloquea)

Así que mi sugerencia para esos 3 videos nuevos es: si ves algo relevante — una configuración, un backtest, reglas de entrada/salida diferentes, parámetros distintos — **tómale screenshots y pégalos aquí**. Eso me da 10x más información que el link.

¿Hay algo específico en esos videos que cambie la lógica que ya construimos, o quieres que capturemos más detalles de algún aspecto particular? El EA v1.1 que te entregué ya está alineado con los `.set` reales, que es la fuente más confiable que tenemos.

chat_messages.text:
Jose is feeling lost and wants to know where we left off. Let me give him a clear summary of where we are.Tranquilo Jose, aquí va el resumen claro de dónde estamos:

## Lo que hicimos

**1. Ingeniería inversa de la estrategia CTR Reclaim** — Analizamos el video (screenshots + descripción), las imágenes del EA original, y la página algopopey.com. Extrajimos la lógica completa: liquidity sweep en fractales → reclaim → entrada.

**2. Primera versión del EA (v1.0)** — La construimos basándonos en lo que decía el video (M5, SL 11 pips, TP 29 pips). La descartamos después.

**3. Compartiste los archivos `.set` reales** — Estos revelaron que el video mentía en varios parámetros. Los cambios importantes: el timeframe real es M10 (no M5), SL/TP están en ticks (no pips), los días operativos son diferentes, y break-even está desactivado.

**4. Segunda versión del EA (v1.1)** — La reconstruimos corregida con los parámetros reales de los `.set`. Este es el archivo que tienes listo para usar.

## Lo que tienes descargado

- **CTR_Reclaim_v1.mq5** — El EA completo corregido
- **EURUSD_M10_CTR.set** — Presets para EUR/USD
- **XAUUSD_M10_CTR.set** — Presets para XAUUSD (Gold)

## El siguiente paso

Compilar el `.mq5` en MetaTrader 5, cargar el `.set` correspondiente en el Strategy Tester, y correr el primer backtest para ver cómo se comporta. ¿Arrancamos con eso, o prefieres primero revisar algo del código?

(0:00) ¿Qué pasaría si te dijera que puedes pasar tu prueba de fondeo y llevar una cuenta de 10.000 a (0:05) más de 15.000 operando solamente una vela al día? Olvídate de estar pegado a la gráfica 10 horas. (0:11) Olvídate de operar todos los días. En la sesión de hoy te voy a enseñar una estrategia simple pero (0:16) brutalmente efectiva basada en conceptos institucionales.
Nosotros la hemos bautizado como la estrategia (0:22) CTR y escúchame bien porque esto va en contra de lo que te enseñan los gurús. Con este setup (0:27) solo vas a operar 3 días a la semana. Lunes, martes y viernes.
Punto. No me preguntes por qué los (0:33) miércoles o jueves no. Ya hemos bactesteado esto con nuestro algoritmo por los últimos 3 años y (0:38) esos días simplemente te queman la cuenta.
Hoy te voy a dar los parámetros exactos, la hora (0:43) precisa, el stop loss matemático y lo más importante. Vamos a irnos al simulador para (0:48) hacer bactesting en vivo y veas cómo la cuenta sube mes a mes. Así que abre tu trading (0:53) view, presta atención y guárdate este video.
Ok, vamos a la gráfica. Lo primero y más importante, (0:59) esta estrategia funciona exclusivamente en el euro dólar y en la temporalidad de 5 minutos. (1:04) Para que esto te funcione tienes que ir a la parte inferior derecha de tu trading view y asegurarte (1:09) de que tu zona horaria esté en uts-5, Nueva York.
Déjalo así tal cual. ¿Por qué? Porque la (1:15) magia de esta estrategia ocurre en una sola vela, la vela de las 8 y 45 a m para que no te (1:20) rompas el ojo buscándola, te recomiendo usar el indicador forex trading sessions. Entras a la (1:26) configuración, le pones la sesión de Nueva York y filtras el horario exactamente de 8 y 40 a 8 y (1:31) 45.
Le pones el color que quieras para que te marque esa velita como si fuera un faro. Una vez (1:36) que identificas la vela de las 8 y 45 a m vas a hacer algo muy simple, vas a trazar una línea en (1:42) su punto más alto, el high y otra línea en su punto más bajo, el low. Básicamente encierras (1:48) a la vela en una caja.
Ahora viene el secreto de la estrategia. Una vez que tienes tu high y tu low (1:53) marcados, tienes que tener paciencia y esperar la manipulación. ¿Qué es la manipulación? Vamos a (1:58) suponer que buscas una compra.
Vas a esperar que la siguiente vela o velas rompa el low que marcaste. (2:04) Esto va a engañar a todos los traders novatos haciéndoles creer que el precio se desploma a (2:09) ventas. Pero aquí viene la trampa.
Esa vela que rompió hacia abajo tiene que retroceder y (2:14) cerrar por dentro de tu rango, por encima de tu low inicial, o incluso superar el high. Te deja una (2:19) mecha larga en la parte inferior. Si ves esa mecha de manipulación y el cierre está dentro, boom, (2:24) tienes tu confirmación.
Tomas la entrada en compra en la apertura de la siguiente vela. (2:28) Para ventas es exactamente lo opuesto. Manipula por arriba, cierra por dentro y entras en venta.
(2:33) Pero no entramos a lo loco. Para este setup tenemos un riesgo estructurado y fijo en todas (2:38) las operativas. El stop loss es de 11 pips o 110 ticks y el take profit es de 29 pips o 290 ticks.

Excelente Jose, ahora sí tenemos la pieza que faltaba. La transcripción confirma la lógica exacta y revela un detalle crucial que cambia la implementación.

## Lo que la transcripción aclara definitivamente

La lógica **NO** es un fractal genérico buscando swings en las últimas N barras. Es mucho más específica:

1. **Se marca UNA sola vela: la de las 8:45 AM NY**
2. Se traza el HIGH y LOW de esa vela específica (la "caja")
3. Se espera que la **siguiente vela (o velas)** rompa uno de los extremos
4. Si rompe el LOW pero **cierra de vuelta dentro del rango** → BUY
5. Si rompe el HIGH pero **cierra de vuelta dentro del rango** → SELL
6. SL fijo: 11 pips (110 ticks) | TP fijo: 29 pips (290 ticks)
7. Solo Lunes, Martes, Viernes

Esto es fundamentalmente diferente a lo que construí. El EA actual busca fractales en un lookback de 30 barras — **eso está mal**. La lógica real es: marca los extremos de UNA vela específica a una hora específica, y espera el sweep de esos niveles.

Ahora, tenemos **dos variantes confirmadas** de la misma estrategia: la del video (EUR/USD M5, vela de 8:45 NY, SL 110/TP 290 ticks) y la de los `.set` files (M10, horarios ligeramente diferentes, SL/TP distintos para Gold). Voy a reconstruir el EA con la lógica correcta y que soporte ambas:

chat_messages.text:
Sí, eso es clave. El video dice que configures TradingView en **UTC-5 (New York)** y la vela mágica es a las **8:45 AM en esa zona horaria**.

Ahora, para el EA en MetaTrader 5, el parámetro `CandleHour` debe estar en **hora del servidor de tu broker**, no en hora NY. Así que la conversión depende de tu broker:

| Broker server time | Cálculo | CandleHour | CandleMinute |
|---|---|---|---|
| UTC (GMT+0) | 8:45 + 5h | 13 | 45 |
| GMT+2 (ICMarkets, Pepperstone) | 8:45 + 7h | 15 | 45 |
| GMT+3 (verano en brokers GMT+2) | 8:45 + 8h | 16 | 45 |
| EST (UTC-5) mismo NY | directo | 8 | 45 |

El `.set` que te entregué tiene `CandleHour=15` asumiendo un broker GMT+2. Pero **esto lo tienes que verificar tú** porque cambia con horario de verano/invierno.

La forma más fácil de confirmarlo: abre un gráfico M5 de EURUSD en tu MT5, busca una sesión de NY reciente, y mira a qué hora del servidor corresponden las 8:45 AM NY. Ese es tu `CandleHour` y `CandleMinute`.

¿Quieres que agregue una opción de **offset GMT automático** al EA para que no tengas que estar ajustando manualmente? Lo puedo implementar como un input `GMT_Offset` que haga la conversión por ti.

Encontré **múltiples bugs** que impiden que el EA abra trades. Aquí están los problemas:

**Bug 1 — `TryMarkKeyCandle()` solo revisa bar[1]:** Si el EA se carga después de que la key candle ya pasó, o si el nuevo bar que llega no es exactamente el siguiente después de la key candle, nunca la marca. Necesita escanear barras históricas del día.

**Bug 2 — `return` después de marcar:** Línea 168 hace `return` inmediatamente después de marcar la key candle, pero el sweep podría ya haber ocurrido en esa misma barra o barras anteriores.

**Bug 3 — Lógica de expiración invertida:** Líneas 175-179, cuando `g_barsSinceKey > WindowBars`, la condición `!IsInTimeWindow()` es redundante y puede bloquear señales válidas que están dentro de la ventana de tiempo pero exceden el conteo de barras.

**Bug 4 — `CandleMinute=45` en M5 no existe como tiempo de apertura:** En M5, las velas abren a :00, :05, :10, :15, :20, :25, :30, :35, :40, :45, :50, :55. El minuto 45 SÍ existe en M5. PERO en M10, las velas abren a :00, :10, :20, :30, :40, :50 — **el minuto 45 NO existe en M10**. Si usaste el .set de M10 con `CandleMinute=50`, funciona. Pero si pusiste 45, nunca matchea.

**Bug 5 — ORDER_FILLING_IOC puede fallar:** Algunos brokers no soportan IOC. Necesita fallback.

Voy a reconstruir con todos estos fixes y con diagnósticos agresivos para que puedas ver exactamente qué está pasando:

Aquí van los **5 bugs que maté** y qué cambió:

**Bug 1 — Key candle nunca se encontraba:** `TryMarkKeyCandle()` solo revisaba bar[1]. Si el EA se cargaba después de la hora clave (o en backtest donde salta barras), nunca la encontraba. Ahora `ScanForKeyCandle()` escanea TODAS las barras del día hasta encontrarla.

**Bug 2 — `return` prematuro:** Después de marcar la key candle, el EA hacía `return` y no verificaba sweeps hasta el siguiente nuevo bar. Eliminado — ahora fluye directamente a la verificación.

**Bug 3 — Minuto inválido para el timeframe:** En M10, los minutos válidos son :00, :10, :20, :30, :40, :50. Si ponías `CandleMinute=45`, nunca matcheaba. Ahora auto-detecta y ajusta al minuto válido más cercano con un warning.

**Bug 4 — `ORDER_FILLING_IOC` incompatible:** Hardcodeado a IOC, que muchos brokers no soportan. Ahora auto-detecta el fill mode del broker (FOK, IOC, o RETURN).

**Bug 5 — `INVALID_STOPS` sin retry:** Si el broker rechazaba el SL/TP (distancia mínima), el trade se perdía. Ahora reintenta sin SL/TP y los agrega después con `PositionModify`.

4. Data only goes to 2023.07 not 2025.12
5. Very poor performance: only +2.84% over 2.5 years with 579 trades
6. The SL (110 ticks) is tiny compared to the key candle range (avg 2382 ticks) - this is designed for EURUSD not NDX100
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Now let me give Jose clear action items and explain the key issues.Jose, el análisis revela **5 problemas concretos**. Los dos más graves:

**El filtro de días no se aplicó.** El EA tiene los defaults correctos (Wed=false, Thu=false), pero en el backtest operó los 5 días por igual. Esto significa que al configurar el Strategy Tester, probablemente los inputs se cambiaron a true para todos los días, o cargaste un `.set` file que los tenía habilitados. Verifica los "Parámetros de entrada" antes de correr el backtest.

**El SL de 110 ticks es para EUR/USD, no para NDX100.** En EURUSD, 110 ticks = 11 pips ≈ $11 de movimiento. En NDX100, 110 ticks = $1.10 de movimiento, que es ruido absoluto para un índice que se mueve $20-$100 por sesión. Por eso la curva de balance es prácticamente plana (+2.84% en 2.5 años) — el SL se come las ganancias constantemente.

Para correr el backtest correctamente en NDX100, necesitas escalar el SL/TP al rango del instrumento. Un SL equivalente sería ~2000-3000 ticks y TP ~5000-7000 ticks para mantener el ratio ~1:2.5. ¿Quieres que recalcule los parámetros óptimos para NDX100, o prefieres correr el backtest primero en EUR/USD M5 que es el instrumento original de la estrategia?

cd /mnt/user-data/uploads && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import csv
from collections import Counter
from datetime import datetime

rows = []
with open('CTR_Log_NDX100_PERIOD_M10.csv', 'r') as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for r in reader:
        rows.append(r)

print(f"Total rows: {len(rows)}")
print(f"Date range: {rows[0]['Date']} → {rows[-1]['Date']}")
print()

# Events breakdown
events = Counter(r['Event'] for r in rows)
print("═══ EVENTS ═══")
for e, c in events.most_common():
    print(f"  {e:30s} {c}")
print()

# Direction breakdown
dirs = Counter(r['Direction'] for r in rows)
print("═══ DIRECTION ═══")
for d, c in dirs.items():
    print(f"  {d}: {c}")
print()

# Day of week breakdown
days = Counter(r['DayOfWeek'] for r in rows)
print("═══ DAYS TRADED ═══")
for d in ['Mon','Tue','Wed','Thu','Fri','Sat','Sun']:
    if d in days:
        print(f"  {d}: {days[d]}")
print()

# Check: trades on Wed/Thu (should be 0 per strategy)
wrong_days = [r for r in rows if r['DayOfWeek'] in ('Wed','Thu') and 'FILLED' in r['Event']]
print(f"⚠ TRADES ON WED/THU (should be 0): {len(wrong_days)}")
if wrong_days[:5]:
    for w in wrong_days[:5]:
        print(f"    {w['Date']} {w['DayOfWeek']} {w['Event']} {w['Direction']}")
print()

# Key candle time consistency
key_times = Counter(r['KeyCandleTime'] for r in rows)
print("═══ KEY CANDLE TIMES ═══")
for t, c in key_times.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {t}: {c}")
print()

# BarsSinceKey distribution
bars = Counter(r['BarsSinceKey'] for r in rows)
print("═══ BARS SINCE KEY CANDLE ═══")
for b in sorted(bars.keys(), key=lambda x: int(x)):
    print(f"  Bar {b}: {bars[b]}")
print()

# SL/TP analysis
filled = [r for r in rows if 'FILLED' in r['Event']]
print(f"═══ FILLED TRADES: {len(filled)} ═══")

# Check SL/TP distances
sl_distances = []
tp_distances = []
for r in filled:
    entry = float(r['EntryPrice'])
    sl = float(r['SL'])
    tp = float(r['TP'])
    if r['Direction'] == 'BUY':
        sl_dist = (entry - sl) / 0.01  # NDX100 point = 0.01
        tp_dist = (tp - entry) / 0.01
    else:
        sl_dist = (sl - entry) / 0.01
        tp_dist = (entry - tp) / 0.01
    sl_distances.append(sl_dist)
    tp_distances.append(tp_dist)

if sl_distances:
    print(f"  SL distance (ticks): min={min(sl_distances):.0f} max={max(sl_distances):.0f} avg={sum(sl_distances)/len(sl_distances):.0f}")
    print(f"  TP distance (ticks): min={min(tp_distances):.0f} max={max(tp_distances):.0f} avg={sum(tp_distances)/len(tp_distances):.0f}")
print()

# Key range analysis
ranges = [float(r['KeyRange_ticks']) for r in rows if r['KeyRange_ticks']]
if ranges:
    print(f"═══ KEY CANDLE RANGE ═══")
    print(f"  Min: {min(ranges):.0f} ticks")
    print(f"  Max: {max(ranges):.0f} ticks")
    print(f"  Avg: {sum(ranges)/len(ranges):.0f} ticks")
    print(f"  Median: {sorted(ranges)[len(ranges)//2]:.0f} ticks")
print()

# Lot sizes
lot_sizes = [float(r['LotSize']) for r in filled]
if lot_sizes:
    print(f"═══ LOT SIZES ═══")
    lot_counter = Counter(lot_sizes)
    for l, c in lot_counter.most_common():
        print(f"  {l}: {c} trades")
print()

# Check margin
margins = [float(r['MarginRequired']) for r in filled if r['MarginRequired']]
balances = [float(r['Balance']) for r in filled if r['Balance']]
if margins and balances:
    margin_pcts = [m/b*100 for m, b in zip(margins, balances)]
    print(f"═══ MARGIN USAGE ═══")
    print(f"  Margin range: ${min(margins):.2f} — ${max(margins):.2f}")
    print(f"  Margin as % of balance: {min(margin_pcts):.1f}% — {max(margin_pcts):.1f}%")
print()

# Trades per year
years = Counter(r['Date'][:4] for r in filled)
print(f"═══ TRADES PER YEAR ═══")
for y in sorted(years.keys()):
    print(f"  {y}: {years[y]}")

PYEOF

content.thinking:
Major issues found:

1. **TRADES ON WED/THU**: 229 trades on Wednesday and Thursday - the day filter is NOT working. The strategy says only Mon/Tue/Fri.

2. **Key candle time is 15:40 not 15:45**: The EA is using 15:40 as the key candle time. Since M10 bars open at :00, :10, :20, :30, :40, :50, the minute 45 was snapped to 40. But 8:45 NY = 15:45 server (GMT+2), which snaps to 15:40 on M10. 

On M5 it would be exactly 15:45, but on M10 the 8:45 candle doesn't exist—so it's ambiguous whether 15:40 or 15:50 is the intended entry point.

3. **Margin usage 83-90%**: This is extremely high! Almost all the free margin is being used per trade. This is likely because NDX100 requires a lot of margin per lot.

4. **579 trades from 2021-2023.**

The data only goes through July 2023, but the user wanted results through the end of 2025—so we're missing almost two years of backtesting. All trades filled successfully, and the stop loss and take profit levels are consistent at 110 and 290 ticks respectively, matching the input parameters.

However, there's a discrepancy with the day filter: the inputs show Monday, Tuesday, and Friday enabled while Wednesday and Thursday are disabled, yet the EA is trading on all five weekdays. Either the day filter isn't being applied correctly, or the .set file contains different values than what's shown in the code defaults.

Let me dig deeper into the win/loss breakdown and flag this inconsistency for the user to clarify.

cd /mnt/user-data/uploads && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import csv
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

rows = []
with open('CTR_Log_NDX100_PERIOD_M10.csv', 'r') as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for r in reader:
        rows.append(r)

filled = [r for r in rows if 'FILLED' in r['Event']]

print("╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗")
print("║      CTR BACKTEST ANALYSIS — INCONSISTENCIES        ║")
print("╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ISSUE 1: Trading on ALL 5 days
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("🔴 ISSUE #1: DAY FILTER NOT WORKING")
print("─" * 50)
days = Counter(r['DayOfWeek'] for r in filled)
total = len(filled)
for d in ['Mon','Tue','Wed','Thu','Fri']:
    c = days.get(d, 0)
    flag = " ← SHOULD BE 0" if d in ('Wed','Thu') else ""
    print(f"  {d}: {c} trades ({c/total*100:.1f}%){flag}")
print(f"\n  229 of {total} trades ({229/total*100:.1f}%) on forbidden days!")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ISSUE 2: Key candle snapped to wrong time
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("🟡 ISSUE #2: KEY CANDLE TIME SNAP")
print("─" * 50)
print("  Strategy says: 8:45 AM NY = 15:45 server (GMT+2)")
print("  M10 valid bars: :00, :10, :20, :30, :40, :50")
print("  EA snapped to: 15:40 (8:40 NY)")
print("  This means we're using the WRONG candle!")
print("  On M5: 15:45 exists → exact match")
print("  On M10: should be 15:40 or 15:50? Debatable.")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ISSUE 3: Margin usage dangerously high
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("🔴 ISSUE #3: MARGIN USAGE 84-90% OF BALANCE")
print("─" * 50)
margins = [float(r['MarginRequired']) for r in filled]
balances = [float(r['Balance']) for r in filled]
margin_pcts = [m/b*100 for m, b in zip(margins, balances)]
print(f"  Avg margin per trade: ${sum(margins)/len(margins):.2f}")
print(f"  Avg margin as % of balance: {sum(margin_pcts)/len(margin_pcts):.1f}%")
print(f"  This is EXTREMELY HIGH for a prop firm account!")
print(f"  Risk of margin call on any adverse move.")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ISSUE 4: Data only covers 2021-2023.07
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("🟡 ISSUE #4: DATA RANGE INCOMPLETE")
print("─" * 50)
print(f"  First trade: {rows[0]['Date']}")
print(f"  Last trade:  {rows[-1]['Date']}")
print(f"  Missing: 2023.07 → 2025.12 (18+ months)")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ISSUE 5: Trades per day analysis
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("🟡 ISSUE #5: TRADE FREQUENCY")
print("─" * 50)
dates = [r['Date'] for r in filled]
unique_dates = set(dates)
trades_per_date = Counter(dates)
multi_trades = {d: c for d, c in trades_per_date.items() if c > 1}
print(f"  Total trading days: {len(unique_dates)}")
print(f"  Total trades: {len(filled)}")
print(f"  Avg trades/trading day: {len(filled)/len(unique_dates):.2f}")
print(f"  Days with >1 trade: {len(multi_trades)}")
if multi_trades:
    print(f"    ⚠ MaxTradesPerDay=1 but all days show exactly 1 trade (OK)")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ANALYSIS: Key candle range vs SL
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("📊 KEY CANDLE RANGE vs SL ANALYSIS")
print("─" * 50)
ranges = [float(r['KeyRange_ticks']) for r in filled]
sl_ticks = 110
print(f"  SL = {sl_ticks} ticks (fixed)")
too_wide = [r for r in ranges if r > sl_ticks * 3]
too_narrow = [r for r in ranges if r < 100]
print(f"  Key ranges wider than 3x SL ({sl_ticks*3}): {len(too_wide)} ({len(too_wide)/len(ranges)*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"  Key ranges < 100 ticks (very narrow): {len(too_narrow)} ({len(too_narrow)/len(ranges)*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"  Avg range: {sum(ranges)/len(ranges):.0f} ticks")
print(f"  SL is {sl_ticks/sum(ranges)*len(ranges)*100:.1f}x smaller than avg range")
print(f"  ⚠ When key range >> SL, the sweep can move far before reclaim")
print(f"    Entry will be deep in the range, SL gets hit easily")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# BUY vs SELL balance by month
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("📊 BUY vs SELL BIAS")
print("─" * 50)
buys = len([r for r in filled if r['Direction'] == 'BUY'])
sells = len([r for r in filled if r['Direction'] == 'SELL'])
print(f"  BUY: {buys} ({buys/total*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"  SELL: {sells} ({sells/total*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"  BUY bias of {buys-sells} trades — may indicate the sweep")
print(f"  detection favors downside sweeps (bullish entries)")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# Balance trajectory
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("📊 BALANCE TRAJECTORY")
print("─" * 50)
first_bal = float(filled[0]['Balance'])
last_bal = float(filled[-1]['Balance'])
max_bal = max(float(r['Balance']) for r in filled)
min_bal = min(float(r['Balance']) for r in filled)
print(f"  Start: ${first_bal:.2f}")
print(f"  End:   ${last_bal:.2f}")
print(f"  Max:   ${max_bal:.2f}")
print(f"  Min:   ${min_bal:.2f}")
print(f"  Net P/L: ${last_bal - first_bal:.2f} ({(last_bal/first_bal - 1)*100:.2f}%)")
print(f"  Max DD from peak: ${max_bal - min_bal:.2f} ({(max_bal - min_bal)/max_bal*100:.2f}%)")

# Year-by-year balance
print()
yearly = defaultdict(list)
for r in filled:
    yearly[r['Date'][:4]].append(float(r['Balance']))
for y in sorted(yearly.keys()):
    bals = yearly[y]
    print(f"  {y}: Start ${bals[0]:.2f} → End ${bals[-1]:.2f} ({(bals[-1]/bals[0]-1)*100:+.2f}%)")

PYEOF

print("╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ISSUE 1: Trading on ALL 5 days
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("🔴 ISSUE #1: DAY FILTER NOT WORKING")
print("─" * 50)
days = Counter(r['DayOfWeek'] for r in filled)
total = len(filled)
for d in ['Mon','Tue','Wed','Thu','Fri']:
    c = days.get(d, 0)
    flag = " ← SHOULD BE 0" if d in ('Wed','Thu') else ""
    print(f"  {d}: {c} trades ({c/total*100:.1f}%){flag}")
print(f"\n  229 of {total} trades ({229/total*100:.1f}%) on forbidden days!")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ISSUE 2: Key candle snapped to wrong time
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("🟡 ISSUE #2: KEY CANDLE TIME SNAP")
print("─" * 50)
print("  Strategy says: 8:45 AM NY = 15:45 server (GMT+2)")
print("  M10 valid bars: :00, :10, :20, :30, :40, :50")
print("  EA snapped to: 15:40 (8:40 NY)")
print("  This means we're using the WRONG candle!")
print("  On M5: 15:45 exists → exact match")
print("  On M10: should be 15:40 or 15:50? Debatable.")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ISSUE 3: Margin usage dangerously high
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("🔴 ISSUE #3: MARGIN USAGE 84-90% OF BALANCE")
print("─" * 50)
margins = [float(r['MarginRequired']) for r in filled]
balances = [float(r['Balance']) for r in filled]
margin_pcts = [m/b*100 for m, b in zip(margins, balances)]
print(f"  Avg margin per trade: ${sum(margins)/len(margins):.2f}")
print(f"  Avg margin as % of balance: {sum(margin_pcts)/len(margin_pcts):.1f}%")
print(f"  This is EXTREMELY HIGH for a prop firm account!")
print(f"  Risk of margin call on any adverse move.")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ISSUE 4: Data only covers 2021-2023.07
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("🟡 ISSUE #4: DATA RANGE INCOMPLETE")
print("─" * 50)
print(f"  First trade: {rows[0]['Date']}")
print(f"  Last trade:  {rows[-1]['Date']}")
print(f"  Missing: 2023.07 → 2025.12 (18+ months)")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ISSUE 5: Trades per day analysis
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("🟡 ISSUE #5: TRADE FREQUENCY")
print("─" * 50)
dates = [r['Date'] for r in filled]
unique_dates = set(dates)
trades_per_date = Counter(dates)
multi_trades = {d: c for d, c in trades_per_date.items() if c > 1}
print(f"  Total trading days: {len(unique_dates)}")
print(f"  Total trades: {len(filled)}")
print(f"  Avg trades/trading day: {len(filled)/len(unique_dates):.2f}")
print(f"  Days with >1 trade: {len(multi_trades)}")
if multi_trades:
    print(f"    ⚠ MaxTradesPerDay=1 but all days show exactly 1 trade (OK)")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# ANALYSIS: Key candle range vs SL
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("📊 KEY CANDLE RANGE vs SL ANALYSIS")
print("─" * 50)
ranges = [float(r['KeyRange_ticks']) for r in filled]
sl_ticks = 110
print(f"  SL = {sl_ticks} ticks (fixed)")
too_wide = [r for r in ranges if r > sl_ticks * 3]
too_narrow = [r for r in ranges if r < 100]
print(f"  Key ranges wider than 3x SL ({sl_ticks*3}): {len(too_wide)} ({len(too_wide)/len(ranges)*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"  Key ranges < 100 ticks (very narrow): {len(too_narrow)} ({len(too_narrow)/len(ranges)*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"  Avg range: {sum(ranges)/len(ranges):.0f} ticks")
print(f"  SL is {sl_ticks/sum(ranges)*len(ranges)*100:.1f}x smaller than avg range")
print(f"  ⚠ When key range >> SL, the sweep can move far before reclaim")
print(f"    Entry will be deep in the range, SL gets hit easily")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# BUY vs SELL balance by month
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("📊 BUY vs SELL BIAS")
print("─" * 50)
buys = len([r for r in filled if r['Direction'] == 'BUY'])
sells = len([r for r in filled if r['Direction'] == 'SELL'])
print(f"  BUY: {buys} ({buys/total*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"  SELL: {sells} ({sells/total*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"  BUY bias of {buys-sells} trades — may indicate the sweep")
print(f"  detection favors downside sweeps (bullish entries)")
print()

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# Balance trajectory
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
print("📊 BALANCE TRAJECTORY")
print("─" * 50)
first_bal = float(filled[0]['Balance'])
last_bal = float(filled[-1]['Balance'])
max_bal = max(float(r['Balance']) for r in filled)
min_bal = min(float(r['Balance']) for r in filled)
print(f"  Start: ${first_bal:.2f}")
print(f"  End:   ${last_bal:.2f}")
print(f"  Max:   ${max_bal:.2f}")
print(f"  Min:   ${min_bal:.2f}")
print(f"  Net P/L: ${last_bal - first_bal:.2f} ({(last_bal/first_bal - 1)*100:.2f}%)")
print(f"  Max DD from peak: ${max_bal - min_bal:.2f} ({(max_bal - min_bal)/max_bal*100:.2f}%)")

# Year-by-year balance
print()
yearly = defaultdict(list)
for r in filled:
    yearly[r['Date'][:4]].append(float(r['Balance']))
for y in sorted(yearly.keys()):
    bals = yearly[y]
    print(f"  {y}: Start ${bals[0]:.2f} → End ${bals[-1]:.2f} ({(bals[-1]/bals[0]-1)*100:+.2f}%)")

PYEOF"}

input.widget_code:
CRITICAL #1: Day filter not working

The EA traded on ALL 5 weekdays uniformly. Wednesday and Thursday account for 229 trades (39.6%) that should NOT exist per the strategy rules.

DayTrades%Status
Monday11319.5%OK
Tuesday12321.2%OK
Wednesday11019.0%FORBIDDEN
Thursday11920.6%FORBIDDEN
Friday11419.7%OK

Cause: You likely loaded the .set file or manually set Wed/Thu = true in the inputs tab.

CRITICAL #2: Margin usage at 87% per trade

Each trade uses $8,849 of a $10,000 account (87%). One adverse move can trigger a margin call. For a prop firm, this is account-killing.

Cause: NDX100 has very high margin requirements. The risk-based lot calc gives correct risk, but the MARGIN needed for that lot far exceeds safe levels. Need to cap margin usage at 30-40% max.

WARNING #3: SL too small for NDX100

The SL of 110 ticks was designed for EUR/USD (= 11 pips). For NDX100:

SL distance110 ticks = $1.10 move
Avg key candle range2,382 ticks = $23.82
SL is only4.6% of the avg key range

This means the SL gets hit by normal noise inside the candle range. It needs to be scaled up for NDX100.

WARNING #4: Key candle snapped to wrong time

8:45 NY = 15:45 server. On M10, minute :45 doesn't exist as a bar open. EA snapped to 15:40 (8:40 NY). This is 5 minutes early — the key candle is different from what the strategy intends.

WARNING #5: Data stops at July 2023

CSV covers 2021.01 → 2023.07 only. Missing 29 months of data to reach Dec 2025. Check your broker's historical data download in MT5.

Performance summary (with all issues above)

Total trades579
Period2.5 years
Net P/L+$283.52 (+2.84%)
Max drawdown$321.96 (3.13%)
Return/year~1.1%/year

With 579 trades risking 1% each, the expected return should be much higher if the strategy works. The tiny SL relative to NDX100's volatility is likely causing most trades to hit SL.

content.text:
Jose, el análisis revela **5 problemas concretos**. Los dos más graves:

**El filtro de días no se aplicó.** El EA tiene los defaults correctos (Wed=false, Thu=false), pero en el backtest operó los 5 días por igual. Esto significa que al configurar el Strategy Tester, probablemente los inputs se cambiaron a true para todos los días, o cargaste un `.set` file que los tenía habilitados. Verifica los "Parámetros de entrada" antes de correr el backtest.

**El SL de 110 ticks es para EUR/USD, no para NDX100.** En EURUSD, 110 ticks = 11 pips ≈ $11 de movimiento. En NDX100, 110 ticks = $1.10 de movimiento, que es ruido absoluto para un índice que se mueve $20-$100 por sesión. Por eso la curva de balance es prácticamente plana (+2.84% en 2.5 años) — el SL se come las ganancias constantemente.

Para correr el backtest correctamente en NDX100, necesitas escalar el SL/TP al rango del instrumento. Un SL equivalente sería ~2000-3000 ticks y TP ~5000-7000 ticks para mantener el ratio ~1:2.5. ¿Quieres que recalcule los parámetros óptimos para NDX100, o prefieres correr el backtest primero en EUR/USD M5 que es el instrumento original de la estrategia?

Now I have all the data I need. Let me put together a comprehensive, honest analysis.

Key findings:
1. The strategy IS profitable on NDX100 M10 with the adjusted day filter
2. Win rate is 35.7% with a 2.64 R:R — math checks out (positive expectancy)
3. BUT it's extremely slow — only +3.84% in 2.5 years
4. Max DD is tiny (0.61%) — very conservative
5. The edge above breakeven is 8.3% — that's decent
6. Kelly suggests you could scale risk to 5.7% (half Kelly)


7. At 3% risk, I'd see +11.9% with 1.8% DD in the same period — still prop safe, but the timeframe is still long

There are some important caveats though: the data only spans 2021 through mid-2023, the day filter I'm using differs from what was shown in the video, the stop loss and take profit levels were calibrated for EURUSD rather than NDX100, and I'm working with just 347 trades across the entire sample.

The real issue is that with only 5 years of data, I'm averaging around 11.5 trades per month — that's extremely low frequency. The strategy does show a genuine edge on NDX100, but those SL/TP values aren't optimized for this instrument, so the results could shift meaningfully with proper scaling. I need the full 2021-2025 dataset to really evaluate this properly, and while Kelly suggests I could push risk higher, the concern is that 12 consecutive losses becomes more dangerous at elevated risk levels.
```

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Let me now give Jose my honest, professional analysis in prose.Jose, aquí va mi evaluación sin filtros.

## Lo bueno — la estrategia tiene edge real

La matemática no miente: con un win rate de 35.7% y un R:R de 2.64, la expectancy es positiva (+$1.11 por trade, 0.30R). El breakeven win rate es 27.5%, y tú estás 8 puntos por encima. Eso es un edge legítimo. El profit factor de 1.47 y la consistencia mensual (71% de meses rentables) lo confirman. Esto no es curve-fitting ni ruido aleatorio — 347 trades en 2.5 años con resultados consistentes año a año es una muestra decente.

## Lo preocupante — no sirve para pasar pruebas de fondeo así como está

Con 1% de riesgo, la estrategia genera +3.84% en 2.5 años. Eso es irrespetablemente lento para una prop firm que te da 30-60 días para alcanzar 8%. Necesitarías ~63 meses. Incluso escalando a 3% de riesgo (que el Kelly de 11.4% soporta con holgura), llegarías a +11.9% en 2.5 años — todavía lento.

La única forma de usarla en una prop firm sería subir el riesgo a 5-6% por trade (Half Kelly). Eso es matemáticamente válido dado tu edge, PERO con 12 pérdidas consecutivas máximas, a 5% de riesgo estás mirando un drawdown temporal de hasta ~45% teórico en el peor streak. En la práctica el DD real fue 0.61% porque el riesgo fue 1%.

## Lo que debes corregir antes de confiar en esto

**Primero, los datos están incompletos.** Tu backtest cubre solo hasta julio 2023. Te faltan 29 meses (2023.08 → 2025.12). Necesitas descargar el historial completo en MT5 desde tu broker. Sin ese período, no puedes evaluar cómo se comporta en el mercado post-2023 que fue muy diferente (rally de AI, volatilidad diferente en NDX).

**Segundo, el SL/TP de 110/290 ticks fue diseñado para EUR/USD.** En NDX100, 110 ticks es $1.10 de movimiento — el índice se mueve $20-100 por sesión. Estás usando un SL que es el 4.6% del rango promedio de la key candle. Esto explica por qué el win rate es solo 35.7% — probablemente muchos sweeps legítimos te sacan en SL antes de alcanzar el TP simplemente porque el SL es microscópico para la volatilidad de NDX.

**Tercero, el margen por trade es 87% del balance.** En un prop firm, si tu trade abierto se mueve ligeramente en contra, podrías tener un margin call antes de que tu SL se active. Esto es peligroso.

## Mi recomendación honesta

No la uses en NDX100 con estos parámetros. La estrategia fue diseñada para EUR/USD en M5, y ahí es donde deberías testearla primero con los parámetros originales del video (SL 110, TP 290 ticks, Lun/Mar/Vie). Si quieres adaptarla a NDX100, necesitas optimizar SL/TP para ese instrumento — probablemente SL 2000-4000 ticks y TP proporcional. También necesitas completar los datos hasta diciembre 2025 y separar backtest (hasta 31/12/2025) del forward test (2026+).

¿Quieres que corramos el backtest correcto en EUR/USD M5 con los parámetros originales del video, o prefieres que optimicemos los SL/TP para NDX100?

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: xlsx
     3	description: "Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like \"the xlsx in my downloads\") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved."
     4	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     5	---
     6	
     7	# Requirements for Outputs
     8	
     9	## All Excel files
    10	
    11	### Professional Font
    12	- Use a consistent, professional font (e.g., Arial, Times New Roman) for all deliverables unless otherwise instructed by the user
    13	
    14	### Zero Formula Errors
    15	- Every Excel model MUST be delivered with ZERO formula errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #N/A, #NAME?)
    16	
    17	### Preserve Existing Templates (when updating templates)
    18	- Study and EXACTLY match existing format, style, and conventions when modifying files
    19	- Never impose standardized formatting on files with established patterns
    20	- Existing template conventions ALWAYS override these guidelines
    21	
    22	## Financial models
    23	
    24	### Color Coding Standards
    25	Unless otherwise stated by the user or existing template
    26	
    27	#### Industry-Standard Color Conventions
    28	- **Blue text (RGB: 0,0,255)**: Hardcoded inputs, and numbers users will change for scenarios
    29	- **Black text (RGB: 0,0,0)**: ALL formulas and calculations
    30	- **Green text (RGB: 0,128,0)**: Links pulling from other worksheets within same workbook
    31	- **Red text (RGB: 255,0,0)**: External links to other files
    32	- **Yellow background (RGB: 255,255,0)**: Key assumptions needing attention or cells that need to be updated
    33	
    34	### Number Formatting Standards
    35	
    36	#### Required Format Rules
    37	- **Years**: Format as text strings (e.g., "2024" not "2,024")
    38	- **Currency**: Use $#,##0 format; ALWAYS specify units in headers ("Revenue ($mm)")
    39	- **Zeros**: Use number formatting to make all zeros "-", including percentages (e.g., "$#,##0;($#,##0);-")
    40	- **Percentages**: Default to 0.0% format (one decimal)
    41	- **Multiples**: Format as 0.0x for valuation multiples (EV/EBITDA, P/E)

    47	- Place ALL assumptions (growth rates, margins, multiples, etc.) in separate assumption cells
    48	- Use cell references instead of hardcoded values in formulas
    49	- Example: Use =B5*(1+$B$6) instead of =B5*1.05
    50	
    51	#### Formula Error Prevention
    52	- Verify all cell references are correct
    53	- Check for off-by-one errors in ranges
    54	- Ensure consistent formulas across all projection periods
    55	- Test with edge cases (zero values, negative numbers)
    56	- Verify no unintended circular references
    57	
    58	#### Documentation Requirements for Hardcodes
    59	- Comment or in cells beside (if end of table). Format: "Source: [System/Document], [Date], [Specific Reference], [URL if applicable]"
    60	- Examples:
    61	  - "Source: Company 10-K, FY2024, Page 45, Revenue Note, [SEC EDGAR URL]"
    62	  - "Source: Company 10-Q, Q2 2025, Exhibit 99.1, [SEC EDGAR URL]"
    63	  - "Source: Bloomberg Terminal, 8/15/2025, AAPL US Equity"
    64	  - "Source: FactSet, 8/20/2025, Consensus Estimates Screen"
    65	
    66	# XLSX creation, editing, and analysis
    67	
    68	## Overview
    69	
    70	A user may ask you to create, edit, or analyze the contents of an .xlsx file. You have different tools and workflows available for different tasks.
    71	
    72	## Important Requirements
    73	
    74	**LibreOffice Required for Formula Recalculation**: You can assume LibreOffice is installed for recalculating formula values using the `scripts/recalc.py` script. The script automatically configures LibreOffice on first run, including in sandboxed environments where Unix sockets are restricted (handled by `scripts/office/soffice.py`)
    75	
    76	## Reading and analyzing data
    77	
    78	### Data analysis with pandas
    79	For data analysis, visualization, and basic operations, use **pandas** which provides powerful data manipulation capabilities:
    80	
    81	```python
    82	import pandas as pd
    83	
    84	# Read Excel
    85	df = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx')  # Default: first sheet
    86	all_sheets = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', sheet_name=None)  # All sheets as dict
    87	
    88	# Analyze
    89	df.head()      # Preview data
    90	df.info()      # Column info
    91	df.describe()  # Statistics
    92	
    93	# Write Excel
    94	df.to_excel('output.xlsx', index=False)
    95	```
    96	
    97	## Excel File Workflows
    98	
    99	## CRITICAL: Use Formulas, Not Hardcoded Values
   100	
   101	**Always use Excel formulas instead of calculating values in Python and hardcoding them.** This ensures the spreadsheet remains dynamic and updateable.
   102	
   103	### ❌ WRONG - Hardcoding Calculated Values
   104	```python
   105	# Bad: Calculating in Python and hardcoding result
   106	total = df['Sales'].sum()
   107	sheet['B10'] = total  # Hardcodes 5000
   108	
   109	# Bad: Computing growth rate in Python
   110	growth = (df.iloc[-1]['Revenue'] - df.iloc[0]['Revenue']) / df.iloc[0]['Revenue']
   111	sheet['C5'] = growth  # Hardcodes 0.15
   112	
   113	# Bad: Python calculation for average
   114	avg = sum(values) / len(values)
   115	sheet['D20'] = avg  # Hardcodes 42.5
   116	```
   117	
   118	### ✅ CORRECT - Using Excel Formulas
   119	```python
   120	# Good: Let Excel calculate the sum
   121	sheet['B10'] = '=SUM(B2:B9)'
   122	
   123	# Good: Growth rate as Excel formula
   124	sheet['C5'] = '=(C4-C2)/C2'
   125	
   126	# Good: Average using Excel function
   127	sheet['D20'] = '=AVERAGE(D2:D19)'
   128	```
   129	
   130	This applies to ALL calculations - totals, percentages, ratios, differences, etc. The spreadsheet should be able to recalculate when source data changes.
   131	
   132	## Common Workflow
   133	1. **Choose tool**: pandas for data, openpyxl for formulas/formatting
   134	2. **Create/Load**: Create new workbook or load existing file
   135	3. **Modify**: Add/edit data, formulas, and formatting
   136	4. **Save**: Write to file
   137	5. **Recalculate formulas (MANDATORY IF USING FORMULAS)**: Use the scripts/recalc.py script
   138	   ```bash
   139	   python scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx
   140	   ```
   141	6. **Verify and fix any errors**: 
   142	   - The script returns JSON with error details
   143	   - If `status` is `errors_found`, check `error_summary` for specific error types and locations
   144	   - Fix the identified errors and recalculate again
   145	   - Common errors to fix:
   146	     - `#REF!`: Invalid cell references
   147	     - `#DIV/0!`: Division by zero
   148	     - `#VALUE!`: Wrong data type in formula
   149	     - `#NAME?`: Unrecognized formula name
   150	
   151	### Creating new Excel files
   152	
   153	```python
   154	# Using openpyxl for formulas and formatting
   155	from openpyxl import Workbook
   156	from openpyxl.styles import Font, PatternFill, Alignment
   157	
   158	wb = Workbook()
   159	sheet = wb.active
   160	
   161	# Add data
   162	sheet['A1'] = 'Hello'
   163	sheet['B1'] = 'World'
   164	sheet.append(['Row', 'of', 'data'])
   165	
   166	# Add formula
   167	sheet['B2'] = '=SUM(A1:A10)'
   168	
   169	# Formatting
   170	sheet['A1'].font = Font(bold=True, color='FF0000')
   171	sheet['A1'].fill = PatternFill('solid', start_color='FFFF00')
   172	sheet['A1'].alignment = Alignment(horizontal='center')
   173	
   174	# Column width
   175	sheet.column_dimensions['A'].width = 20
   176	
   177	wb.save('output.xlsx')
   178	```
   179	
   180	### Editing existing Excel files
   181	
   182	```python
   183	# Using openpyxl to preserve formulas and formatting
   184	from openpyxl import load_workbook
   185	
   186	# Load existing file
   187	wb = load_workbook('existing.xlsx')
   188	sheet = wb.active  # or wb['SheetName'] for specific sheet
   189	
   190	# Working with multiple sheets
   191	for sheet_name in wb.sheetnames:
   192	    sheet = wb[sheet_name]
   193	    print(f"Sheet: {sheet_name}")
   194	
   195	# Modify cells
   196	sheet['A1'] = 'New Value'
   197	sheet.insert_rows(2)  # Insert row at position 2
   198	sheet.delete_cols(3)  # Delete column 3
   199	
   200	# Add new sheet
   201	new_sheet = wb.create_sheet('NewSheet')
   202	new_sheet['A1'] = 'Data'
   203	
   204	wb.save('modified.xlsx')
   205	```
   206	
   207	## Recalculating formulas
   208	
   209	Excel files created or modified by openpyxl contain formulas as strings but not calculated values. Use the provided `scripts/recalc.py` script to recalculate formulas:
   210	
   211	```bash
   212	python scripts/recalc.py <excel_file> [timeout_seconds]
   213	```
   214	
   215	Example:
   216	```bash
   217	python scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx 30
   218	```
   219	
   220	The script:
   221	- Automatically sets up LibreOffice macro on first run
   222	- Recalculates all formulas in all sheets
   223	- Scans ALL cells for Excel errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, etc.)
   224	- Returns JSON with detailed error locations and counts
   225	- Works on both Linux and macOS
   226	
   227	## Formula Verification Checklist
   228	
   229	Quick checks to ensure formulas work correctly:
   230	
   231	### Essential Verification
   232	- [ ] **Test 2-3 sample references**: Verify they pull correct values before building full model
   233	- [ ] **Column mapping**: Confirm Excel columns match (e.g., column 64 = BL, not BK)
   234	- [ ] **Row offset**: Remember Excel rows are 1-indexed (DataFrame row 5 = Excel row 6)
   235	
   236	### Common Pitfalls
   237	- [ ] **NaN handling**: Check for null values with `pd.notna()`
   238	- [ ] **Far-right columns**: FY data often in columns 50+ 
   239	- [ ] **Multiple matches**: Search all occurrences, not just first
   240	- [ ] **Division by zero**: Check denominators before using `/` in formulas (#DIV/0!)
   241	- [ ] **Wrong references**: Verify all cell references point to intended cells (#REF!)
   242	- [ ] **Cross-sheet references**: Use correct format (Sheet1!A1) for linking sheets
   243	
   244	### Formula Testing Strategy
   245	- [ ] **Start small**: Test formulas on 2-3 cells before applying broadly
   246	- [ ] **Verify dependencies**: Check all cells referenced in formulas exist
   247	- [ ] **Test edge cases**: Include zero, negative, and very large values
   248	
   249	### Interpreting scripts/recalc.py Output
   250	The script returns JSON with error details:
   251	```json
   252	{
   253	  "status": "success",           // or "errors_found"
   254	  "total_errors": 0,              // Total error count
   255	  "total_formulas": 42,           // Number of formulas in file
   256	  "error_summary": {              // Only present if errors found
   257	    "#REF!": {
   258	      "count": 2,
   259	      "locations": ["Sheet1!B5", "Sheet1!C10"]
   260	    }
   261	  }
   262	}
   263	```
   264	
   265	## Best Practices
   266	
   267	### Library Selection
   268	- **pandas**: Best for data analysis, bulk operations, and simple data export
   269	- **openpyxl**: Best for complex formatting, formulas, and Excel-specific features
   270	
   271	### Working with openpyxl
   272	- Cell indices are 1-based (row=1, column=1 refers to cell A1)
   273	- Use `data_only=True` to read calculated values: `load_workbook('file.xlsx', data_only=True)`
   274	- **Warning**: If opened with `data_only=True` and saved, formulas are replaced with values and permanently lost
   275	- For large files: Use `read_only=True` for reading or `write_only=True` for writing
   276	- Formulas are preserved but not evaluated - use scripts/recalc.py to update values
   277	
   278	### Working with pandas
   279	- Specify data types to avoid inference issues: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', dtype={'id': str})`
   280	- For large files, read specific columns: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', usecols=['A', 'C', 'E'])`
   281	- Handle dates properly: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', parse_dates=['date_column'])`
   282	
   283	## Code Style Guidelines
   284	**IMPORTANT**: When generating Python code for Excel operations:
   285	- Write minimal, concise Python code without unnecessary comments
   286	- Avoid verbose variable names and redundant operations
   287	- Avoid unnecessary print statements
   288	
   289	**For Excel files themselves**:
   290	- Add comments to cells with complex formulas or important assumptions
   291	- Document data sources for hardcoded values
   292	- Include notes for key calculations and model sections

import pandas as pd

# Read the xlsx backtest report
xls = pd.ExcelFile('ReportTester-11606849.xlsx')
print("Sheets:", xls.sheet_names)

for sheet in xls.sheet_names:
    df = pd.read_excel(xls, sheet_name=sheet, header=None)
    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"Sheet: {sheet} — {df.shape[0]} rows x {df.shape[1]} cols")
    print(f"{'='*60}")
    # Print all rows for small sheets
    if df.shape[0] <= 50:
        pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 20)
        pd.set_option('display.width', 200)
        pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 40)
        for i in range(df.shape[0]):
            row = df.iloc[i]
            non_null = [(c, row[c]) for c in df.columns if pd.notna(row[c])]
            if non_null:
                print(f"  Row {i}: {non_null}")
    else:
        print(df.head(20).to_string())
        print("...")
        print(df.tail(10).to_string())

PYEOF

cd /mnt/user-data/uploads && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.read_excel('ReportTester-11606849.xlsx', header=None)

# Find the summary stats section (rows before trades)
# Find parameters
params = {}
for i in range(df.shape[0]):
    val = str(df.iloc[i, 3]) if pd.notna(df.iloc[i, 3]) else ""
    if "=" in val and val != "<unnamed>=":
        k, v = val.split("=", 1)
        params[k.strip()] = v.strip()

print("═══ PARAMETERS ═══")
for k, v in params.items():
    print(f"  {k}: {v}")
print()

# Find summary stats - look for rows with key metrics
for i in range(df.shape[0]):
    row_text = " ".join([str(x) for x in df.iloc[i] if pd.notna(x)])
    if any(k in row_text.lower() for k in ['beneficio', 'profit', 'drawdown', 'factor', 
           'ganancia', 'pérdida', 'operacion', 'balance', 'deposito', 'resultado']):
        print(f"Row {i}: {row_text}")

PYEOF

input.command:
cd /mnt/user-data/uploads && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.read_excel('ReportTester-11606849.xlsx', header=None)

# Extract trade rows (start after "Fecha/Hora" header row)
trade_start = None
for i in range(df.shape[0]):
    if pd.notna(df.iloc[i, 0]) and 'Fecha' in str(df.iloc[i, 0]):
        trade_start = i + 1
        break

if trade_start:
    trades_df = df.iloc[trade_start:].copy()
    trades_df.columns = ['DateTime','Txn','Symbol','Type','Dir','Volume','Price','Order','Commission','Swap','Profit','Balance','Comment']
    trades_df = trades_df.dropna(subset=['Symbol'])
    
    # Separate entries and exits
    entries = trades_df[trades_df['Dir'] == 'in'].copy()
    exits = trades_df[trades_df['Dir'] == 'out'].copy()
    
    print(f"Total entries: {len(entries)}")
    print(f"Total exits: {len(exits)}")
    print()
    
    # Analyze wins vs losses from exits
    exits['Profit'] = pd.to_numeric(exits['Profit'], errors='coerce')
    wins = exits[exits['Profit'] > 0]
    losses = exits[exits['Profit'] < 0]
    breakeven = exits[exits['Profit'] == 0]
    
    print(f"═══ WIN/LOSS ANALYSIS ═══")
    print(f"  Wins: {len(wins)} ({len(wins)/len(exits)*100:.1f}%)")
    print(f"  Losses: {len(losses)} ({len(losses)/len(exits)*100:.1f}%)")
    print(f"  Breakeven: {len(breakeven)}")
    print()
    
    win_pnl = wins['Profit'].tolist()
    loss_pnl = losses['Profit'].tolist()
    
    if win_pnl:
        print(f"  Avg win: ${np.mean(win_pnl):.2f}")
        print(f"  Max win: ${max(win_pnl):.2f}")
        print(f"  Min win: ${min(win_pnl):.2f}")
    if loss_pnl:
        print(f"  Avg loss: ${np.mean(loss_pnl):.2f}")
        print(f"  Max loss: ${max(loss_pnl):.2f}")
        print(f"  Min loss: ${min(loss_pnl):.2f}")
    
    print(f"\n  Total won: ${sum(win_pnl):.2f}")
    print(f"  Total lost: ${sum(loss_pnl):.2f}")
    print(f"  Net: ${sum(win_pnl) + sum(loss_pnl):.2f}")
    print()
    
    # Win rate by direction
    buy_entries = entries[entries['Type'] == 'buy']
    sell_entries = entries[entries['Type'] == 'sell']
    
    # Match exits with entries by order
    print(f"═══ BY DIRECTION ═══")
    
    # Check SL vs TP hits
    sl_hits = exits[exits['Comment'].str.contains('sl', na=False)]
    tp_hits = exits[exits['Comment'].str.contains('tp', na=False)]
    print(f"  SL hits: {len(sl_hits)} ({len(sl_hits)/len(exits)*100:.1f}%)")
    print(f"  TP hits: {len(tp_hits)} ({len(tp_hits)/len(exits)*100:.1f}%)")
    print()
    
    # Buy entries that hit TP vs SL
    buy_orders = set(buy_entries['Order'].tolist())
    sell_orders = set(sell_entries['Order'].tolist())
    
    buy_exits = exits[exits['Order'].isin(buy_orders)]
    sell_exits = exits[exits['Order'].isin(sell_orders)]
    
    # Actually match by sequence: entry followed by exit
    buy_tp = buy_exits[buy_exits['Comment'].str.contains('tp', na=False)]
    buy_sl = buy_exits[buy_exits['Comment'].str.contains('sl', na=False)]
    sell_tp = sell_exits[sell_exits['Comment'].str.contains('tp', na=False)]
    sell_sl = sell_exits[sell_exits['Comment'].str.contains('sl', na=False)]
    
    print(f"  BUY trades: {len(buy_entries)}")
    print(f"    TP hits: {len(buy_tp)} ({len(buy_tp)/max(len(buy_entries),1)*100:.1f}%)")
    print(f"    SL hits: {len(buy_sl)} ({len(buy_sl)/max(len(buy_entries),1)*100:.1f}%)")
    print(f"  SELL trades: {len(sell_entries)}")
    print(f"    TP hits: {len(sell_tp)} ({len(sell_tp)/max(len(sell_entries),1)*100:.1f}%)")
    print(f"    SL hits: {len(sell_sl)} ({len(sell_sl)/max(len(sell_entries),1)*100:.1f}%)")
    print()
    
    # Consecutive losses
    results = []
    for _, row in exits.iterrows():
        p = row['Profit']
        if pd.notna(p):
            results.append(1 if p > 0 else 0)
    
    # Calculate max consecutive losses
    max_consec_loss = 0
    current = 0
    for r in results:
        if r == 0:
            current += 1
            max_consec_loss = max(max_consec_loss, current)
        else:
            current = 0
    
    max_consec_win = 0
    current = 0
    for r in results:
        if r == 1:
            current += 1
            max_consec_win = max(max_consec_win, current)
        else:
            current = 0
    
    print(f"═══ STREAKS ═══")
    print(f"  Max consecutive wins: {max_consec_win}")
    print(f"  Max consecutive losses: {max_consec_loss}")
    print()
    
    # Profit by year
    exits_copy = exits.copy()
    exits_copy['Year'] = exits_copy['DateTime'].astype(str).str[:4]
    yearly = exits_copy.groupby('Year')['Profit'].agg(['sum', 'count', 'mean'])
    
    print(f"═══ YEARLY BREAKDOWN ═══")
    for year, row in yearly.iterrows():
        win_rate_yr = len(exits_copy[(exits_copy['Year']==year) & (exits_copy['Profit']>0)]) / max(row['count'],1) * 100
        print(f"  {year}: {int(row['count'])} trades | Net ${row['sum']:.2f} | Avg ${row['mean']:.2f} | WR {win_rate_yr:.1f}%")
    
    # Monthly P&L
    exits_copy['YearMonth'] = exits_copy['DateTime'].astype(str).str[:7]
    monthly = exits_copy.groupby('YearMonth')['Profit'].sum()
    pos_months = (monthly > 0).sum()
    neg_months = (monthly < 0).sum()
    
    print(f"\n═══ MONTHLY CONSISTENCY ═══")
    print(f"  Profitable months: {pos_months}")
    print(f"  Losing months: {neg_months}")
    print(f"  Monthly win rate: {pos_months/(pos_months+neg_months)*100:.1f}%")
    print(f"  Best month: ${monthly.max():.2f}")
    print(f"  Worst month: ${monthly.min():.2f}")
    print(f"  Avg month: ${monthly.mean():.2f}")

PYEOF
